%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# Stability

> **Section Objective:** Decide discrete-time system stability by reading the poles of the transfer function relative to the unit circle, while remembering what transfer functions can and cannot reveal.

## Concepts In This Section

- BIBO stability from poles
- External vs internal stability
- Pole-zero cancellation caveat
- Asymptotic stability
- Instability
- Marginal stability

## 1. BIBO Stability from the Poles of H[z]

\(H[z]\) and \(h[n]\) describe the system externally — at its input and output terminals — without looking inside.

If every pole \(p_k\) of \(H[z]\) lies **strictly inside the unit circle**, each corresponding impulse-response term behaves like a decaying exponential. The entire impulse response \(h[n]\) is then absolutely summable, which is exactly the condition for BIBO stability.

**Symbol meanings:**
- \(p_k\) — a pole of \(H[z]\)
- \(|p_k|\) — its distance from the origin in the \(z\)-plane

**Use case:** Apply this when an exam gives \(H[z]\) or a pole-zero plot and asks for BIBO stability.

**Common misuse:** Do not treat a pole on the unit circle (\(|p_k| = 1\)) as inside it.

**Minimal example:** Poles at \(0.5\) and \(-0.8\) both satisfy \(|p_k| < 1\), so the system is BIBO-stable.

$$\text{BIBO stable} \Longleftrightarrow |p_k|<1\text{ for every pole }p_k\text{ of }H[z]$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

## 2. External Stability Is Not Always Internal Stability

\(H[z]\) is an **external** description of the system — it only sees what enters and exits. The denominator of \(H[z]\) looks like the characteristic polynomial \(Q[z]\), but this equality is only guaranteed when the numerator \(P[z]\) and \(Q[z]\) share **no common factors**.

If \(P[z]\) and \(Q[z]\) have a common factor, that factor cancels in \(H[z]\). The canceled root disappears from the visible transfer function, hiding an internal mode. \(H[z]\) can then only determine **external BIBO stability** — it cannot confirm internal stability.

**Symbol meanings:**
- \(P[z]\) — numerator polynomial of \(H[z]\)
- \(Q[z]\) — polynomial from the system's internal description
- Characteristic roots — roots of \(Q[z]\)

**Exam trigger:** If a question mentions pole-zero cancellation or asks whether transfer-function poles equal characteristic roots, apply this caveat.

**Common misuse:** Assuming every canceled factor is harmless for internal stability. A canceled unstable root is still an internal instability.

$$\text{No common factors in }P[z]\text{ and }Q[z]\Longrightarrow \{\text{poles of }H[z]\}=\{\text{characteristic roots}\}$$

## 3. Internal Asymptotic Stability When No Cancellation Is Hiding Modes

Once \(P[z]\) and \(Q[z]\) have no common factors, the poles of \(H[z]\) are exactly the system's characteristic roots. The internal stability test can then be read directly from the pole locations.

The formula means every characteristic mode must decay — each pole must lie strictly inside the unit circle. **Repeated poles are allowed** as long as they are still strictly inside the unit circle; repetition inside does not cause instability.

**Use case:** Apply this when the problem explicitly states there are no cancellations, gives a minimal transfer function, or asks for internal or asymptotic stability.

**Common misuse:** Rejecting a repeated pole at \(|p_k| < 1\) as unstable. Repetition is only dangerous at the boundary \(|p_k| = 1\).

**Minimal example:** A double pole at \(0.7\) satisfies \(|0.7| < 1\), so the system is asymptotically stable.

$$\text{asymptotically stable} \Longleftrightarrow |p_k|<1\text{ for every pole }p_k$$

## 4. Instability and the Unit-Circle Boundary

An LTID system is **unstable** under either of two conditions:

1. At least one pole lies **outside** the unit circle (\(|p_k| > 1\)), because that mode grows without bound.
2. A pole lies **on** the unit circle (\(|p_k| = 1\)) and is **repeated**, because the repeated boundary mode also grows.

**Symbol:** \(\exists\) means "there exists at least one."

**Use case:** Apply this when an exam gives a list of poles and asks you to distinguish unstable from marginally stable.

**Common misuse:** Saying every unit-circle pole is unstable. The boundary rule is more precise:
- **Simple** poles on the unit circle → marginal stability (not unstable)
- **Repeated** poles on the unit circle → unstable

The radius alone is not enough; you must also check multiplicity at \(|p_k| = 1\).

$$\text{unstable} \Longleftrightarrow \exists\, p_k: |p_k|>1\text{ or a repeated pole has }|p_k|=1$$

## 5. Marginal Stability: The Easy Boundary Trap

A system is **marginally stable** when all three conditions hold simultaneously:

1. No pole is outside the unit circle: \(|p_k| \leq 1\) for all \(k\).
2. At least one pole lies exactly on the unit circle: \(|p_k| = 1\) for some \(k\).
3. Every unit-circle pole is **simple** (multiplicity 1).

**Minimal example:** Poles at \(0.4\) and \(e^{j\pi/3}\). Since \(|0.4| < 1\) and \(|e^{j\pi/3}| = 1\) with the unit-circle pole simple, the system is marginally stable.

### NEAR-MISS — EXAM TRAP

If \(e^{j\pi/3}\) is a **repeated** pole, the system is **unstable**, not marginally stable. The only change is multiplicity at the boundary, but the classification flips entirely.

> **Rule:** Simple unit-circle pole → marginal. Repeated unit-circle pole → unstable. The distinction is multiplicity, not radius.

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- \(|p_k|<1\) for every pole \(p_k\) gives BIBO stability from \(H[z]\); a pole on or outside the unit circle breaks this.
- No common factors in \(P[z]\) and \(Q[z]\) means poles of \(H[z]\) equal characteristic roots; cancellation can hide internal modes.
- Asymptotic stability: \(|p_k|<1\) for every pole; repeated poles strictly inside the unit circle are still asymptotically stable.
- Unstable if \(\exists\, p_k: |p_k|>1\) or a repeated pole has \(|p_k|=1\); simple unit-circle poles imply marginal boundary behavior.

*Next, inverse systems use transfer functions to ask whether one system can undo another.*
